expanded_description:
The real exchange rate (RER) is the nominal exchange rate adjusted for differences in price levels between countries. While the nominal exchange rate tells you how many units of foreign currency you can buy with one unit of domestic currency, the real exchange rate tells you how many units of foreign goods you can buy with one unit of domestic goods. In other words, it measures the relative price of foreign goods in terms of domestic goods. The real exchange rate is a key determinant of a country's international competitiveness: a real depreciation (fall in RER) makes domestic goods cheaper relative to foreign goods, boosting exports; a real appreciation (rise in RER) makes domestic goods more expensive, reducing exports.

Understanding the real exchange rate is essential for analyzing trade flows, competitiveness, and the effects of inflation differentials on exchange rates. Unlike the nominal exchange rate, which can be highly volatile due to financial flows, the real exchange rate tends to be more stable in the long run, converging toward purchasing power parity (PPP). Deviations from PPP can persist for years due to trade barriers, transportation costs, and differences in productivity growth (Balassa-Samuelson effect).

In this section, we will define the real exchange rate, derive its relationship to the nominal exchange rate and price levels, analyze the determinants of real exchange rate movements, discuss the concept of purchasing power parity, explore the Balassa-Samuelson effect, and examine the implications for competitiveness and trade policy. You will learn to calculate real exchange rates, interpret changes, and understand why real exchange rates matter more than nominal rates for trade.

core_concept.principle:
The real exchange rate (RER) is the nominal exchange rate adjusted for price level differences between countries. It measures the relative price of foreign goods in terms of domestic goods and is a key determinant of international competitiveness.

Definition:

$RER = E \times \frac{P^*}{P}$

where:
• $E$ = nominal exchange rate (units of domestic currency per unit of foreign currency)
• $P^*$ = foreign price level (CPI or GDP deflator)
• $P$ = domestic price level

Alternatively, if $E$ is defined as units of foreign currency per unit of domestic currency (common in many textbooks), then $RER = \frac{E \times P}{P^*}$. The interpretation is symmetric.

Interpretation:

• $RER > 1$: Foreign goods are more expensive than domestic goods (domestic goods are competitive).
• $RER = 1$: Domestic and foreign goods cost the same (purchasing power parity holds).
• $RER < 1$: Foreign goods are cheaper than domestic goods (domestic goods are less competitive).

Real Appreciation vs. Real Depreciation:

• Real appreciation (RER ↑): Domestic goods become more expensive relative to foreign goods. Competitiveness worsens. Can be caused by nominal appreciation or higher domestic inflation.
• Real depreciation (RER ↓): Domestic goods become cheaper relative to foreign goods. Competitiveness improves. Can be caused by nominal depreciation or lower domestic inflation.

Relationship to Nominal Exchange Rate and Inflation:

Taking logs and differentiating:

$\%\Delta RER = \%\Delta E + \pi^* - \pi$

where $\pi$ and $\pi^*$ are domestic and foreign inflation rates. A country with higher inflation than its trading partners will experience a real appreciation (unless its nominal exchange rate depreciates enough to offset).

Purchasing Power Parity (PPP):

• Absolute PPP: $RER = 1$ (law of one price for a basket of goods).
• Relative PPP: $\%\Delta E = \pi - \pi^*$ (nominal exchange rate changes offset inflation differentials, keeping RER constant).
• PPP does not hold in the short run due to trade barriers, transportation costs, and non-traded goods.
• In the long run, RER tends to mean-revert toward PPP, but deviations can persist for years.

Balassa-Samuelson Effect:

Countries with higher productivity growth in the traded goods sector tend to have stronger real exchange rates (real appreciation). This is because productivity gains lead to higher wages, which spill over into non-traded goods, raising the overall price level. Richer countries tend to have higher price levels (the Penn effect).

Real Exchange Rate and Competitiveness:

• A real depreciation makes exports cheaper and imports more expensive, improving the trade balance (Marshall-Lerner condition).
• A real appreciation makes exports more expensive and imports cheaper, worsening the trade balance.
• Policymakers sometimes try to achieve a real depreciation to boost exports (competitive devaluation), but this can lead to currency wars.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine you want to compare the cost of living between the United States and Canada. The nominal exchange rate tells you that 1 US dollar buys 1.35 Canadian dollars. But that doesn't tell you whether goods are cheaper in the US or Canada. If a US hamburger costs $5 and a Canadian hamburger costs $6 CAD, then the real exchange rate is $1.35 \times (6/5) = 1.62$. This means that Canadian hamburgers are 62% more expensive than US hamburgers when converted to USD. The US has a competitive advantage in hamburgers.

If the Canadian dollar depreciates (USD/CAD rises to 1.45), but Canadian inflation is 2% higher than US inflation, the real depreciation may be partially offset. The real exchange rate captures both the nominal change and the inflation differential.

In the long run, real exchange rates tend to move toward purchasing power parity. If the US has higher inflation than Canada, the US dollar should depreciate to keep US goods competitive. If it doesn't, US goods become more expensive (real appreciation), and the US will run a trade deficit.

The Balassa-Samuelson effect explains why rich countries often have stronger real exchange rates. Switzerland has very high productivity in traded goods (pharmaceuticals, watches, finance). This drives up wages, which spill over into non-traded goods (haircuts, restaurant meals), raising the overall price level. The Swiss franc is overvalued relative to PPP, but this is consistent with the country's high productivity.

Central banks monitor the real exchange rate because it affects inflation (through import prices) and trade competitiveness. The Bank of Canada pays attention to the CAD real exchange rate, as it influences Canadian exports (30% of GDP).

image.alt: Graph: Real exchange rate (USD/CAD adjusted for inflation) vs. nominal exchange rate; real rate is less volatile than nominal rate; long-run mean reversion toward PPP.
image.caption: Figure 12.2.1: The real exchange rate adjusts the nominal rate for price level differences. It is less volatile than the nominal rate and tends to mean-revert toward purchasing power parity in the long run.

sections.introduction:
The real exchange rate (RER) is the nominal exchange rate adjusted for differences in price levels between countries. While the nominal exchange rate tells you how many units of foreign currency you can buy with one unit of domestic currency, the real exchange rate tells you how many units of foreign goods you can buy with one unit of domestic goods. In other words, it measures the relative price of foreign goods in terms of domestic goods. The real exchange rate is a key determinant of a country's international competitiveness: a real depreciation (fall in RER) makes domestic goods cheaper relative to foreign goods, boosting exports; a real appreciation (rise in RER) makes domestic goods more expensive, reducing exports.

Understanding the real exchange rate is essential for analyzing trade flows, competitiveness, and the effects of inflation differentials on exchange rates. Unlike the nominal exchange rate, which can be highly volatile due to financial flows, the real exchange rate tends to be more stable in the long run, converging toward purchasing power parity (PPP). Deviations from PPP can persist for years due to trade barriers, transportation costs, and differences in productivity growth (Balassa-Samuelson effect).

In this section, we will define the real exchange rate, derive its relationship to the nominal exchange rate and price levels, analyze the determinants of real exchange rate movements, discuss the concept of purchasing power parity, explore the Balassa-Samuelson effect, and examine the implications for competitiveness and trade policy.

introduction_image.alt: Graph: Real exchange rate (USD/CAD) and Canada-US trade balance; correlation shows real depreciation improves trade balance.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 12.2.2: The real exchange rate is closely related to trade competitiveness. A real depreciation (lower RER) tends to improve the trade balance.

key_concept.definition:
Real Exchange Rate (RER): The nominal exchange rate adjusted for price level differences between countries. It measures the relative price of foreign goods in terms of domestic goods.

Formula:

$RER = E \times \frac{P^*}{P}$

where:
• $E$ = nominal exchange rate (units of domestic currency per unit of foreign currency)
• $P^*$ = foreign price level (CPI or GDP deflator)
• $P$ = domestic price level

Interpretation:

• $RER > 1$: Foreign goods are more expensive than domestic goods (domestic goods competitive).
• $RER = 1$: Purchasing power parity holds.
• $RER < 1$: Foreign goods are cheaper than domestic goods (domestic goods less competitive).

Real Appreciation vs. Real Depreciation:

• Real appreciation (RER ↑): Domestic goods become more expensive relative to foreign goods. Competitiveness worsens.
• Real depreciation (RER ↓): Domestic goods become cheaper relative to foreign goods. Competitiveness improves.

Growth Rate Relationship:

$\%\Delta RER = \%\Delta E + \pi^* - \pi$

A country with higher inflation than its trading partners will experience a real appreciation unless its nominal exchange rate depreciates sufficiently.

Purchasing Power Parity (PPP):

• Absolute PPP: $RER = 1$ (law of one price for a basket of goods).
• Relative PPP: $\%\Delta E = \pi - \pi^*$ (nominal depreciation offsets inflation differential).
• PPP holds in the long run but not in the short run due to trade barriers, transportation costs, and non-traded goods.

Balassa-Samuelson Effect:

Countries with higher productivity growth in traded goods tend to have stronger real exchange rates (real appreciation). Richer countries have higher price levels (the Penn effect).

Determinants of Real Exchange Rates:

• Productivity differentials: Higher productivity leads to real appreciation (Balassa-Samuelson).
• Terms of trade: Improvement in terms of trade (export prices rise relative to import prices) leads to real appreciation.
• Government spending: Higher government spending can lead to real appreciation (if it falls on non-traded goods).
• Capital flows: Capital inflows lead to nominal appreciation, which may lead to real appreciation if prices are sticky.
• Monetary policy: Expansionary monetary policy leads to nominal depreciation, which may lead to real depreciation in the short run.

Real Exchange Rate and Trade Balance:

• A real depreciation improves the trade balance if the Marshall-Lerner condition holds (sum of export and import demand elasticities > 1).
• The J-curve effect: real depreciation initially worsens the trade balance before improving it (due to contracts and adjustment lags).
key_concept.context: The real exchange rate concept emerged from the purchasing power parity literature. Gustav Cassel (1918) introduced PPP. The Balassa-Samuelson effect (1964) explained why PPP fails in cross-country comparisons. The real exchange rate is a key variable in open-economy macroeconomics, appearing in models of trade balance determination, exchange rate overshooting (Dornbusch, 1976), and competitiveness. Central banks monitor real exchange rates to gauge competitiveness and to forecast inflation (through import prices).

image.alt: Diagram: RER = E × (P*/P); arrows showing nominal exchange rate and price levels as inputs.
image.caption: Figure 12.2.3: The real exchange rate is calculated by multiplying the nominal exchange rate by the ratio of foreign to domestic price levels.

classifications.value: Real appreciation makes domestic goods more expensive relative to foreign goods, hurting competitiveness; real depreciation improves competitiveness
characteristics: Real appreciation (RER ↑): Domestic goods become more expensive. Can be caused by nominal appreciation or higher domestic inflation.
Real depreciation (RER ↓): Domestic goods become cheaper. Can be caused by nominal depreciation or lower domestic inflation.
Real appreciation hurts exporters (their goods become more expensive abroad) but benefits consumers (imports become cheaper).
Real depreciation benefits exporters but hurts consumers (imports become more expensive).
Central banks may try to achieve real depreciation to boost exports (competitive devaluation).
Sustained real appreciation can lead to de-industrialization (Dutch disease).
classifications.behavior:
Real appreciation occurs when domestic goods become more expensive relative to foreign goods. This can happen in two ways: (1) the nominal exchange rate appreciates (the domestic currency becomes stronger), or (2) domestic inflation is higher than foreign inflation, and the nominal exchange rate does not depreciate enough to offset.

For example, if Canada has 3% inflation while the US has 2% inflation, and the CAD/USD exchange rate remains constant, then Canadian goods become more expensive relative to US goods. The real exchange rate appreciates, hurting Canadian exporters.

Real depreciation is the opposite: domestic goods become cheaper. This can happen through nominal depreciation or lower domestic inflation. A weaker CAD makes Canadian goods cheaper for Americans, boosting exports.

The Dutch disease is a phenomenon where a country experiences sustained real appreciation due to a natural resource boom (e.g., oil). The currency appreciates, making other tradable sectors (manufacturing) uncompetitive. Canada has experienced this with oil: when oil prices rise, the CAD appreciates, hurting Ontario's manufacturing sector.

Central banks sometimes intervene to prevent excessive real appreciation. The Swiss National Bank (SNB) intervened massively to prevent the Swiss franc from appreciating too much, as a strong franc would hurt Swiss exporters. The SNB even introduced a negative interest rate to discourage capital inflows.

image.alt: Graph: Real exchange rate (USD/CAD) rising (real appreciation) and falling (real depreciation); corresponding effects on exports.
image.caption: Figure 12.2.4: Real appreciation (RER ↑) makes domestic goods more expensive, hurting exports; real depreciation (RER ↓) makes domestic goods cheaper, boosting exports.

examples.explanation: From 2002 to 2007, the CAD appreciated from USD/CAD = 1.60 to 1.10 (a 31% nominal appreciation). Canadian inflation was slightly higher than US inflation, so the real appreciation was even larger. Canadian exporters (manufacturing, forestry) struggled; many jobs were lost in Ontario and Quebec. This was partly due to the Dutch disease (oil prices rising).

examples.process: Real Depreciation after 2014 Oil Price Collapse
examples.explanation: Oil prices fell from $100 to $30 in 2014-2016. The CAD depreciated from USD/CAD = 1.10 to 1.40 (a 27% nominal depreciation). Canadian inflation was lower than US inflation, so the real depreciation was significant. Canadian exporters (manufacturing) benefited, and the trade balance improved.

classifications.value: PPP states that in the long run, the real exchange rate should equal 1 (or be constant). It is a long-run anchor for exchange rates
characteristics: Absolute PPP: RER = 1 (law of one price for a basket of goods).
Relative PPP: %ΔE = π - π* (nominal depreciation equals inflation differential).
PPP does not hold in the short run due to trade barriers, transportation costs, non-traded goods, and sticky prices.
PPP holds better in the long run (over decades) and for high-inflation countries.
The Big Mac Index is a lighthearted test of PPP.
Deviations from PPP can persist for years (the 'PPP puzzle').
classifications.behavior:
Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) is a theory that exchange rates should adjust so that the same basket of goods costs the same in different countries when converted to a common currency. Absolute PPP implies that the real exchange rate should equal 1. Relative PPP implies that the real exchange rate should be constant (not necessarily 1), with nominal exchange rate changes offsetting inflation differentials.

In reality, PPP does not hold in the short run. Trade barriers (tariffs, quotas), transportation costs, and non-traded goods (haircuts, housing, restaurant meals) prevent arbitrage. Even for traded goods, prices can differ due to market segmentation, pricing to market, and sticky prices.

However, PPP holds better in the long run. Studies show that real exchange rates tend to mean-revert, with a half-life of 3-5 years. For countries with very high inflation (hyperinflation), PPP holds almost perfectly in the short run because nominal exchange rates adjust quickly.

The Big Mac Index, published by The Economist, compares the price of a Big Mac across countries. If a Big Mac costs $5 in the US and 6.50 CHF in Switzerland, PPP implies an exchange rate of 1.30 CHF/USD. If the actual rate is 0.90, the Swiss franc is overvalued. The Big Mac Index is a fun way to illustrate PPP, but it is not a precise measure (Big Macs are not identical across countries, and prices include non-traded components like rent and labor).

image.alt: Map showing Big Mac prices in various countries; overvalued and undervalued currencies relative to USD.
image.caption: Figure 12.2.5: The Big Mac Index illustrates purchasing power parity. Currencies that are overvalued relative to PPP (e.g., Swiss franc) may be expected to depreciate in the long run.

examples.process: Big Mac Index: Swiss Franc Overvaluation
examples.explanation: In 2023, a Big Mac cost $5.00 in the US and 6.50 CHF in Switzerland. PPP-implied exchange rate = 6.50/5.00 = 1.30 CHF/USD. Actual exchange rate was about 0.90 CHF/USD, meaning the Swiss franc was overvalued by 44% relative to PPP. This suggests that the franc may depreciate in the long run (though it has been overvalued for years due to safe-haven flows).

During hyperinflation, nominal exchange rates adjust almost instantly to inflation differentials. PPP holds almost perfectly in the short run because prices and exchange rates change daily. In Zimbabwe, the exchange rate depreciated in line with inflation, so the real exchange rate remained relatively stable.

classifications.value: Higher productivity in traded goods leads to real appreciation; richer countries have stronger real exchange rates
characteristics: Productivity growth in traded goods sector raises wages.
Higher wages spill over into non-traded goods sector (services, construction), raising the overall price level.
Richer countries (Switzerland, Norway, US) have higher price levels than poorer countries (China, India, Mexico).
The Penn effect: price levels are positively correlated with GDP per capita.
Balassa-Samuelson explains why PPP fails in cross-country comparisons.
classifications.behavior:
The Balassa-Samuelson effect explains why rich countries tend to have stronger real exchange rates (higher price levels) than poor countries. The mechanism: productivity growth in the traded goods sector (manufacturing, agriculture) is higher in rich countries. This raises wages in the traded sector. Because labor is mobile, wages in the non-traded sector (services, construction, retail) also rise. Since non-traded goods are produced with less productivity growth, their prices rise relative to traded goods. The overall price level (which includes both traded and non-traded goods) is higher in rich countries.

For example, Switzerland has very high productivity in traded goods (pharmaceuticals, watches, finance). Wages are high. A haircut in Zurich costs much more than a haircut in Manila, even though productivity in haircutting is similar. The overall price level in Switzerland is higher, so the Swiss franc is overvalued relative to PPP. This is not a market failure; it is an equilibrium outcome of productivity differences.

The Balassa-Samuelson effect implies that as countries develop, their real exchange rates tend to appreciate. China's real exchange rate has appreciated significantly as its productivity has grown. This is a normal part of economic development.

For Canada, the Balassa-Samuelson effect suggests that the CAD may appreciate over time as productivity grows, but the effect is moderated by Canada's commodity price cycle (which can cause large swings in the real exchange rate).

image.alt: Scatter plot: GDP per capita (x-axis) vs. price level (y-axis); positive correlation (Penn effect).
image.caption: Figure 12.2.6: The Balassa-Samuelson effect: richer countries have higher price levels (stronger real exchange rates). This explains why PPP fails in cross-country comparisons.

examples.explanation: As China's productivity has grown rapidly since 2000, its real exchange rate has appreciated significantly. The nominal RMB/USD exchange rate has been managed (pegged until 2005, then crawling), but inflation has been higher in China than in the US, leading to real appreciation. Chinese goods have become more expensive, and China's trade surplus has declined as a share of GDP.

Switzerland has one of the highest GDP per capita in the world. The Swiss franc is consistently overvalued relative to PPP. This is partly due to Balassa-Samuelson (high productivity) and partly due to safe-haven flows (investors buy francs during crises).

principles.principle: The Real Exchange Rate, Not the Nominal Rate, Determines Competitiveness
principles.statement: A country's international competitiveness depends on the relative price of its goods compared to foreign goods. The nominal exchange rate alone is misleading because price levels differ across countries. A country could have a depreciated nominal exchange rate but high inflation, making its goods no cheaper in real terms. Conversely, a country could have an appreciated nominal rate but low inflation, making its goods competitive. For example, Japan had a strong yen in the 1980s, but Japanese goods remained competitive because Japanese inflation was low. The real exchange rate captures both effects. Policymakers should focus on the real exchange rate when assessing competitiveness and trade flows. A real depreciation (fall in RER) will improve the trade balance if the Marshall-Lerner condition holds. A real appreciation will worsen the trade balance.
principles.application: When evaluating a country's competitiveness, look at the real exchange rate, not the nominal rate. Central banks and finance ministries monitor real effective exchange rates (REER), which are trade-weighted averages of real exchange rates against multiple trading partners. A sustained real appreciation may signal a loss of competitiveness and a need for structural reforms (productivity improvements) or a weaker currency. Canada's real exchange rate is heavily influenced by oil prices; policymakers should look through temporary fluctuations but respond to persistent trends.
principles.example: In the early 2000s, the US dollar depreciated nominally against the euro, but US goods remained competitive because US inflation was low. The real exchange rate did not depreciate as much as the nominal rate. In the 1970s, the US dollar depreciated nominally, but high US inflation meant that US goods were not competitive; the real exchange rate actually appreciated. This is why nominal exchange rates alone are misleading.

image.alt: Graph: Real exchange rate (USD/EUR) and US trade balance; real depreciation improves trade balance; real appreciation worsens it.
image.caption: Figure 12.2.7: The real exchange rate, not the nominal rate, determines competitiveness. A real depreciation improves the trade balance; a real appreciation worsens it.

principles.principle: Real Exchange Rates Tend to Mean-Revert in the Long Run, but Deviations Can Be Persistent
principles.standard: Empirical studies show that real exchange rates are mean-reverting: they tend to return toward purchasing power parity over time. The half-life of deviations is estimated to be 3-5 years. However, deviations can persist for much longer due to productivity differentials (Balassa-Samuelson), trade barriers, and sticky prices. For example, the Swiss franc has been overvalued relative to PPP for decades due to safe-haven flows and high productivity. The Chinese yuan was undervalued for years due to managed exchange rates and rapid productivity growth. Policymakers should not expect real exchange rates to adjust quickly to PPP; long-term trends are often driven by fundamentals. The PPP puzzle—why real exchange rates deviate for so long—remains an active research area.
principles.application: Investors and policymakers should be cautious about assuming that overvalued currencies will depreciate quickly. Real exchange rates can remain overvalued for years if supported by fundamentals (productivity, safe-haven demand, commodity prices). The Swiss National Bank's intervention to cap the franc's appreciation (2011-2015) was a response to persistent overvaluation. For Canada, the real exchange rate is heavily influenced by oil prices; when oil prices are high, the CAD is strong, and this is an equilibrium outcome (not a misalignment).
principles.example: The Japanese yen was overvalued relative to PPP in the 1990s and 2000s due to deflation (falling prices in Japan). The yen remained strong even as the Japanese economy stagnated. The real exchange rate eventually depreciated in the 2010s after Abenomics (monetary easing) led to higher inflation expectations.

image.alt: Graph: Real exchange rate (USD/JPY) showing mean reversion over decades; deviations persist for years.
image.caption: Figure 12.2.8: Real exchange rates tend to mean-revert toward purchasing power parity in the long run, but deviations can persist for years or even decades.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Real Exchange Rates and Changes

image.alt: Table: E=1.35, P_us=100, P_canada=120; RER=1.62; after changes: E=1.40, P_us=102, P_canada=125; RER=1.715; percentage change calculations.
image.caption: Figure 12.2.9: Worked example: calculating the real exchange rate and the effects of nominal and price changes.

worked_examples.problem:
The nominal exchange rate is USD/CAD = 1.35. The US CPI is 100, and the Canadian CPI is 120.

a) Calculate the real exchange rate (RER) with the US as the domestic country.
b) Interpret the RER: are US goods cheaper or more expensive than Canadian goods?
c) If the USD appreciates to 1.40 (USD/CAD rises), US CPI rises to 102, and Canadian CPI rises to 125, calculate the new RER.
d) Calculate the percentage change in the nominal exchange rate, US inflation, Canadian inflation, and the real exchange rate.
e) Verify the relationship: %ΔRER = %ΔE + π_canada - π_us.

step_by_step.explanation: RER = 1.62 > 1 means that Canadian goods are 62% more expensive than US goods (when converted to USD). US goods are cheaper relative to Canadian goods. The US has a competitive advantage in trade with Canada.
$\%\Delta RER = \%\Delta E + \pi_{Canada} - \pi_{US} = 3.70\% + 4.167\% - 2.0\% = 5.867\%$ (close to 5.91%, small rounding difference). The relationship holds.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) RER = 1.62. b) Canadian goods 62% more expensive; US goods cheaper. c) RER' = 1.7157. d) %ΔE = 3.70%, π_US = 2.0%, π_Canada = 4.167%, %ΔRER = 5.91%. e) Verified.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the real exchange rate formula and the relationship between nominal exchange rate changes, inflation differentials, and real exchange rate changes.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Competitiveness and Trade Balance

image.alt: Table: Country A (RER=1.2, exports as % of GDP=40%), Country B (RER=0.8, exports=25%); analysis of competitiveness.
image.caption: Figure 12.2.10: Worked example: analyzing competitiveness using real exchange rates and trade balance effects.

worked_examples.problem:
Two countries, A and B, trade with each other. Country A has a real exchange rate (RER) of 1.2 (foreign goods relative to domestic). Country B has a real exchange rate of 0.8 (same definition, but careful: RER is defined from each country's perspective).

a) Which country's goods are more competitive?
b) Which country would you expect to have a trade surplus?
c) If Country A experiences a real depreciation of 10%, what is the expected effect on its trade balance (assuming Marshall-Lerner condition holds)?
d) If Country B has higher inflation than Country A, and the nominal exchange rate does not adjust, what happens to the real exchange rates?
e) How could Country A achieve a real depreciation without changing the nominal exchange rate?

step_by_step.explanation:
For Country A, RER = 1.2 means foreign goods are 20% more expensive than domestic goods. Country A's goods are competitive.

For Country B, RER = 0.8 means foreign goods are 20% cheaper than domestic goods. Country B's goods are less competitive (foreign goods cheaper).

Country A has more competitive goods (its goods are cheaper relative to foreign goods).

Country A (competitive) would be expected to export more and import less, so it should have a trade surplus. Country B (less competitive) would be expected to have a trade deficit.

However, other factors (income levels, trade policies, exchange rate regimes) also matter. But all else equal, the country with the lower RER (more competitive) should have a trade surplus.

If Country A experiences a real depreciation of 10% (RER falls from 1.2 to 1.08), its goods become even cheaper relative to foreign goods. Assuming the Marshall-Lerner condition holds (sum of export and import demand elasticities > 1), the trade balance should improve (exports rise, imports fall). The J-curve effect suggests that the trade balance may initially worsen before improving, as contracts adjust slowly.

If Country B has higher inflation than Country A, and the nominal exchange rate does not adjust, then the real exchange rate for Country A will change. Using the formula %ΔRER_A = %ΔE + π_B - π_A. If %ΔE = 0 (nominal rate fixed) and π_B > π_A, then %ΔRER_A > 0. Country A's real exchange rate appreciates (RER rises), making its goods less competitive. Country B's real exchange rate depreciates (since from B's perspective, RER_B = 1/RER_A). So Country B becomes more competitive.

This is why countries with fixed exchange rates often experience real appreciation if they have higher inflation than the anchor country.

step_by_step.step: Achieving real depreciation without nominal change
step_by_step.explanation:
Country A could achieve a real depreciation without changing the nominal exchange rate by having lower inflation than its trading partners. If Country A's inflation is lower than Country B's, then even with a fixed nominal exchange rate, Country A's goods become cheaper in real terms (real depreciation). This is why countries sometimes try to engineer real depreciations through tight monetary policy (low inflation) rather than nominal devaluation.

Alternatively, Country A could increase productivity in the traded goods sector (Balassa-Samuelson effect leads to real appreciation, not depreciation). To get real depreciation, Country A would need to lower its price level relative to foreign countries, which is difficult (deflation).

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Country A (RER=1.2 means its goods are cheaper). b) Country A expected to have trade surplus. c) Trade balance improves (if ML condition holds). d) Country A real appreciates (competitiveness worsens); Country B real depreciates. e) Lower inflation than trading partners (tight monetary policy).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying real exchange rate analysis to assess competitiveness, predict trade balances, and understand the effects of inflation differentials.

process_flow.process: Obtain nominal exchange rate (E) and price levels (P, P*)
process_flow.description: Collect data on the nominal exchange rate and consumer price indices for the domestic and foreign countries.
process_flow.mechanism: E from forex markets; P and P* from statistical agencies (CPI, GDP deflator).

image.alt: Diagram: Data sources (forex, CPI) → E, P, P*.
image.caption: Figure 12.2.11: Step 1 - Collect data on the nominal exchange rate and price levels from statistical agencies.

process_flow.process: Calculate real exchange rate (RER = E × P*/P)
process_flow.description: Multiply the nominal exchange rate by the ratio of foreign to domestic price levels.

image.caption: Figure 12.2.12: Step 2 - Calculate the real exchange rate using the formula.

process_flow.description: If RER > 1, foreign goods are more expensive (domestic goods competitive). If RER < 1, foreign goods are cheaper (domestic goods less competitive).

image.caption: Figure 12.2.13: Step 3 - Interpret the real exchange rate: RER > 1 indicates domestic goods are competitive; RER < 1 indicates foreign goods are cheaper.

process_flow.description: Track changes in RER over time to determine whether competitiveness is improving or worsening.

image.caption: Figure 12.2.14: Step 4 - Compute the percentage change in the real exchange rate to determine real appreciation or depreciation.

process_flow.process: Assess implications for trade balance and policy
process_flow.description: Use the RER to assess competitiveness and predict trade balance effects; consider policy responses if RER is misaligned.
process_flow.mechanism: Real depreciation → trade balance improvement (if ML condition holds); real appreciation → trade balance worsening.

image.alt: Diagram: Real depreciation → improves trade balance; real appreciation → worsens trade balance; policy options (intervention, monetary policy).
image.caption: Figure 12.2.15: Step 5 - Assess the implications of real exchange rate changes for the trade balance and consider appropriate policy responses.

image.alt: Master framework: Collect data (E, P, P*) → calculate RER → interpret competitiveness → compute changes → assess trade balance implications.
image.caption: Figure 12.2.16: Complete framework for analyzing real exchange rates.

summary: The real exchange rate (RER) is the nominal exchange rate adjusted for price level differences: $RER = E \times P^*/P$. It measures the relative price of foreign goods in terms of domestic goods and is the key determinant of international competitiveness. A real depreciation (RER ↓) makes domestic goods cheaper relative to foreign goods, boosting exports; a real appreciation (RER ↑) makes domestic goods more expensive, reducing exports. The relationship between changes in the nominal exchange rate, inflation differentials, and the real exchange rate is $\%\Delta RER = \%\Delta E + \pi^* - \pi$. Purchasing power parity (PPP) states that in the long run, the real exchange rate should equal 1 (absolute PPP) or be constant (relative PPP), but PPP fails in the short run due to trade barriers, transportation costs, and non-traded goods. The Balassa-Samuelson effect explains why richer countries have stronger real exchange rates: higher productivity in traded goods raises wages, which spill over into non-traded goods, raising the overall price level. Real exchange rates tend to mean-revert toward PPP in the long run, but deviations can persist for years. Understanding the real exchange rate is essential for analyzing trade flows, competitiveness, and the effects of inflation differentials on exchange rates.
